quizQuestion: Who invented Quicksort in 1959 while a visiting student in Moscow, making it one of the most widely used sorting algorithms ever?
explanation: Tony Hoare conceived Quicksort in 1959 while at Moscow State University and published it in 1961. The algorithm uses divide-and-conquer: selecting a pivot, partitioning elements into smaller/larger groups, and recursing. Hoare later called his invention of the null reference a 'billion-dollar mistake'.
visualDescription: Array of colored blocks being rapidly partitioned around a glowing pivot element by a recursive divide-and-conquer pattern

quizQuestion: Merge Sort was invented in 1945 by a computing pioneer famous for the architecture inside nearly every modern computer. Who was it?
explanation: John von Neumann invented Merge Sort in 1945. The algorithm uses divide-and-conquer: recursively splitting the array, sorting each half, then merging. It guarantees O(n log n) in all cases and is stable, making it a cornerstone sorting algorithm.
visualDescription: A branching binary tree of array segments being split apart and then recombined in sorted order, like a symmetrical data waterfall

quizQuestion: What Python core developer invented Timsort in 2002, a hybrid algorithm that became the default sort in Python and Java?
explanation: Tim Peters created Timsort in 2002 for Python. It is a hybrid of Merge Sort and Insertion Sort that exploits natural 'runs' (already-sorted sequences) in real-world data. Python has used it as its default sort since version 2.3, and Java adopted it for object arrays in Java 7.
visualDescription: The Python and Java logos flanking a sorted array where naturally-ordered 'runs' are highlighted in different colors before merging

quizQuestion: Who invented Heapsort in 1964, publishing it in the Communications of the ACM and also introducing the binary heap data structure?
explanation: J.W.J. Williams invented Heapsort in 1964 (Algorithm 232 in CACM). Robert W. Floyd refined the algorithm in the same year. Heapsort uses a binary heap to repeatedly extract the maximum element, achieving guaranteed O(n log n) in all cases with O(1) auxiliary space.
visualDescription: A binary heap represented as a pyramid of numbered blocks, with the maximum element being repeatedly extracted from the top

quizQuestion: What is the average-case time complexity of Bubble Sort, the 'simple but slow' algorithm that repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order elements?
explanation: Bubble Sort has O(n²) average and worst-case complexity. It repeatedly compares and swaps adjacent elements, causing smaller values to 'bubble' upward. While easy to understand and implement, it is far too slow for large datasets and is primarily used for teaching.
visualDescription: A row of numbered blocks with arrows showing adjacent swaps, smaller elements bubbling upward like carbonation

quizQuestion: Merge Sort is one of the few sorting algorithms that guarantees the same time complexity in ALL cases — best, average, and worst. What is it?
explanation: Merge Sort runs in O(n log n) in all cases — best, average, and worst. Unlike Quicksort, it never degrades to O(n²). This guarantee, combined with its stability, makes it the preferred choice for sorting linked lists and external (disk-based) sorting.
visualDescription: Three identical complexity graphs — best, average, and worst — all showing the same n log n curve, highlighted green

quizQuestion: Binary Search is dramatically faster than linear search on sorted arrays. What is its time complexity?
explanation: Binary Search has O(log n) time complexity. By repeatedly halving the search interval, it finds a target in a sorted array far faster than scanning linearly. Searching one billion sorted items requires at most 30 comparisons. It requires the array to be sorted first.
visualDescription: A sorted row of 1,024 blocks with a glowing cursor halving the search space repeatedly until zeroing in on the target

quizQuestion: Quicksort is fast in practice but has a nasty worst case on already-sorted arrays with a poor pivot choice. What is that worst-case complexity?
explanation: Quicksort's worst-case time complexity is O(n²), occurring when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (e.g. on an already-sorted array with naive pivot selection). In practice, randomized or median-of-three pivot selection avoids this. Average case remains O(n log n).
visualDescription: A sorted array with the first element chosen as pivot each time, creating a deeply lopsided partition tree that degrades to linear scanning

quizQuestion: Depth-First Search (DFS) explores as far as possible down each branch before backtracking. What data structure — or its implicit equivalent in recursion — powers this?
explanation: DFS uses a stack (or the implicit call stack in recursion). It pushes nodes to visit and pops the most recently added, exploring deep paths before backtracking. DFS is used for topological sorting, cycle detection, maze-solving, and connected components detection.
visualDescription: A graph with arrows plunging deep into one branch before backtracking, with a stack data structure on the side tracking the path

quizQuestion: Breadth-First Search (BFS) finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph by exploring all neighbors level by level. What data structure makes this possible?
explanation: BFS uses a queue (FIFO). Nodes to visit are added to the back and processed from the front, ensuring all vertices at distance k are explored before any at distance k+1. This guarantees the shortest path (fewest edges) is found first.
visualDescription: A graph with nodes lit up level by level outward from the source like ripples in water, with a queue tracking the frontier

quizQuestion: In what year was A* Search published by Hart, Nilsson, and Raphael at SRI International, giving game AI and GPS the most famous pathfinding algorithm ever?
explanation: A* was published in 1968 by Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael at SRI International. It combines Dijkstra's actual-cost tracking with a heuristic estimate of remaining distance. A* is optimal when the heuristic never overestimates (admissible heuristic). It dominates game AI pathfinding to this day.
visualDescription: A grid maze with a glowing optimal path highlighted, numbers showing the combined g+h cost estimate at each explored cell

quizQuestion: Who invented the shortest-path algorithm in 1956 that powers GPS navigation and internet routing protocols to this day?
explanation: Edsger W. Dijkstra conceived his shortest-path algorithm in 1956 and published it in 1959. He reportedly designed it in 20 minutes as a demonstration of his thinking. The algorithm finds the shortest path from a source to all other nodes in a weighted graph, but only works with non-negative edge weights.
visualDescription: A weighted graph with glowing shortest-path lines radiating outward from a source node, distances labeled at each vertex

quizQuestion: In what year did Dijkstra publish his famous shortest-path algorithm in Numerische Mathematik?
explanation: Dijkstra conceived his algorithm in 1956 but published it in 1959 in 'Numerische Mathematik' ('A note on two problems in connexion with graphs'). It remains one of the most cited algorithms in computer science.
visualDescription: The cover of a 1959 mathematics journal with a hand-drawn graph sketched in the margins

quizQuestion: Dijkstra's Algorithm fails on certain graph types, but the Bellman-Ford Algorithm handles them correctly. What does Bellman-Ford support that Dijkstra cannot?
explanation: Bellman-Ford handles graphs with negative-weight edges. Dijkstra's greedy approach breaks when negative edges exist. Bellman-Ford is slower — O(V×E) vs Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V) — but can detect negative-weight cycles and is used in distance-vector routing protocols like RIP.
visualDescription: Two graphs side by side: one with all positive weights where both algorithms succeed, and one with a negative-weight edge where only Bellman-Ford finds the correct path

quizQuestion: Joseph Kruskal's 1956 algorithm sorts edges by weight and greedily adds them without forming cycles. What problem does it solve?
explanation: Kruskal's Algorithm finds the minimum spanning tree (MST) of a weighted undirected graph — the set of edges that connects all vertices with the minimum total weight. It uses a Union-Find data structure for cycle detection. MSTs are used in network design, clustering, and approximation algorithms.
visualDescription: A weighted undirected graph with the minimum spanning tree edges highlighted in green, while heavier redundant edges remain grey

quizQuestion: The Floyd-Warshall Algorithm finds shortest paths between ALL pairs of vertices using dynamic programming. What is its time complexity?
explanation: Floyd-Warshall runs in O(V³) time and O(V²) space. Published by Robert Floyd and Stephen Warshall in 1962, it uses dynamic programming to consider each vertex as a potential intermediate node. It handles negative-weight edges (but not negative cycles) and is well-suited for dense graphs.
visualDescription: An adjacency matrix being iteratively updated with shortest-path distances as each vertex is considered as an intermediate node

quizQuestion: A stack data structure follows what access pattern — the last element added is the first one removed?
explanation: A stack uses LIFO (Last In, First Out) ordering. Core operations are push (add to top), pop (remove from top), and peek (view top without removing). Stacks power function call frames, undo operations, expression parsing, and Depth-First Search.
visualDescription: A vertical stack of colored blocks with an arrow pushing one on top, and another arrow removing from the top only

quizQuestion: A queue data structure — used in BFS and task scheduling — follows what access pattern?
explanation: A queue uses FIFO (First In, First Out) ordering. Elements are enqueued at the rear and dequeued from the front. This mirrors a real-world queue of people. Queues are fundamental to BFS graph traversal, print spoolers, message passing systems, and CPU task scheduling.
visualDescription: A horizontal row of colored blocks with arrows adding to the right end and removing from the left end, like people in a checkout line

quizQuestion: Hash tables are used in Python dicts and JavaScript objects for a reason. What is the average-case time complexity for lookup, insertion, and deletion?
explanation: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time for lookup, insertion, and deletion. A hash function maps keys to array indices, enabling near-instant access. Worst case degrades to O(n) if many keys collide. Python's dict and JavaScript's object are both hash tables under the hood.
visualDescription: Keys being fed through a hash function into colored buckets in an array, with arrows showing O(1) direct access to the correct bucket

quizQuestion: A balanced Binary Search Tree (BST) stores values so that every left subtree is smaller and every right subtree is larger. What is its average-case search complexity?
explanation: A balanced BST has O(log n) average-case search, insert, and delete. Each comparison halves the remaining search space. However, if the tree becomes unbalanced (degenerate), worst case degrades to O(n). Self-balancing variants like AVL and Red-Black trees guarantee O(log n) worst case.
visualDescription: A symmetrical binary tree with a highlighted search path zigzagging down through left/right comparisons to find a target value

quizQuestion: In 1936, who proved that no algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will halt or run forever — a result that shook the foundations of mathematics?
explanation: Alan Turing proved the undecidability of the halting problem in his 1936 paper 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem.' Using a clever diagonal argument, he showed that no general algorithm can decide whether any arbitrary program halts. This established fundamental limits on what computers can ever compute.
visualDescription: A 1936-era Turing Machine diagram with an infinite tape, a question mark replacing the halting state — undecidable by any algorithm

quizQuestion: In what year did both Alonzo Church (lambda calculus) and Alan Turing (Turing machines) independently publish their landmark models of computation?
explanation: Both Alonzo Church and Alan Turing published their foundational models of computation in 1936. Church proposed lambda calculus; Turing proposed the abstract Turing machine. They independently arrived at the same conclusion — now called the Church-Turing thesis — that their models define exactly what is 'computable'.
visualDescription: Two blackboards side by side: one covered in lambda calculus notation, the other showing a Turing machine diagram, both dated 1936

quizQuestion: The P vs. NP problem is one of the greatest unsolved questions in computer science. How much does the Clay Mathematics Institute offer for its solution?
explanation: The Clay Mathematics Institute offers $1,000,000 for a solution to P vs. NP — one of its seven Millennium Prize Problems. The question, formalized by Stephen Cook in 1971, asks whether every problem whose solution can be quickly verified can also be quickly solved. Most believe P ≠ NP, but neither direction has been proved.
visualDescription: A chalkboard with 'P = NP ?' in enormous letters, with a million-dollar prize plaque mounted below it

quizQuestion: Which 19th-century mathematician created the algebra of TRUE and FALSE values that underlies all digital logic and every computer ever built?
explanation: George Boole created Boolean algebra in his 1847 work 'The Mathematical Analysis of Logic' and expanded it in 'The Laws of Thought' (1854). Claude Shannon showed in 1937 that Boolean algebra could describe electrical circuits, bridging abstract math and physical computing hardware.
visualDescription: A Victorian-era mathematician's portrait beside a modern logic gate circuit diagram, connected by a two-sided arrow labeled 'AND, OR, NOT'

acceptableAlternatives: Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman
quizQuestion: RSA encryption is named after its inventors' initials. Which three MIT researchers published the first practical public-key cryptosystem in 1977?
explanation: RSA was published in 1977 by Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman at MIT — the acronym comes from their surnames. Its security rests on the difficulty of factoring the product of two large prime numbers. Unknown to them, GCHQ's Clifford Cocks had discovered an equivalent algorithm in 1973, but it remained classified until 1997.
visualDescription: Three MIT ID badges labeled R, S, and A beside a padlock icon and mathematical notation involving large prime numbers

quizQuestion: PageRank is named after one of its creators. Which two Stanford PhD students invented it in 1998 and used it to build the world's dominant search engine?
explanation: Larry Page and Sergey Brin developed PageRank at Stanford University in 1998 and used it as the foundation of Google's search engine. The algorithm ranks web pages by the number and quality of incoming links — a link from a highly-ranked page transfers more authority. The name comes from Larry Page, not 'web page'.
visualDescription: A web graph with nodes of different sizes representing link authority, arrows flowing between them, the largest node labeled 'Google'

quizQuestion: Which two Google engineers published the MapReduce paper in 2004, revealing how Google processed petabytes of data across thousands of machines?
explanation: Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat published 'MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters' at OSDI 2004. The programming model splits work into Map (produce key-value pairs) and Reduce (aggregate) phases. Apache Hadoop (2006) brought the open-source implementation that powered much of the big data era.
visualDescription: A distributed cluster of servers with Map workers processing chunks in parallel, then Reduce workers combining results into a final output

quizQuestion: The Fast Fourier Transform, published by Cooley and Tukey in 1965, was revolutionary because it reduced DFT computation from O(n²) to what?
explanation: The FFT reduces Discrete Fourier Transform computation from O(n²) to O(n log n) — a massive improvement for large inputs. Called one of the most important algorithms of the 20th century, it enables digital signal processing, JPEG image compression, MP3 audio, and telecommunications. Gauss had discovered equivalent methods around 1805 but never published them properly.
visualDescription: A before/after complexity graph showing an O(n²) curve towering over an O(n log n) curve, with signal wave diagrams below

quizQuestion: In what year did Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams publish their landmark Nature paper popularizing Backpropagation for training neural networks?
explanation: The landmark backpropagation paper by David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams was published in Nature in 1986. This paper popularized the algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks. Earlier work by Paul Werbos (1974) and Linnainmaa (1970) had laid foundations, but the 1986 paper sparked widespread adoption.
visualDescription: A multi-layer neural network diagram with error signals flowing backward through the layers as arrows, with 'Nature 1986' stamped across the top

quizQuestion: The Cook-Levin Theorem (1971) proved the first NP-complete problem, opening the floodgates for Karp's 21 problems in 1972. What was that first NP-complete problem?
explanation: Stephen Cook's 1971 theorem proved that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete — the first such proof. Richard Karp then showed 21 other classic problems (including Travelling Salesman, Graph Coloring, and Knapsack) reduce to NP-complete. If anyone solves any NP-complete problem in polynomial time, P = NP.
visualDescription: A complex Boolean formula with AND/OR/NOT gates connected in a circuit, with a question mark asking whether any variable assignment satisfies it

quizQuestion: Big-O notation is fundamental to algorithm analysis, but it was not invented by a computer scientist. Who introduced it in an 1894 mathematics book?
explanation: Paul Bachmann introduced Big-O notation in his 1894 book 'Die Analytische Zahlentheorie.' Edmund Landau later popularized it — which is why it is also called 'Landau notation' or 'Bachmann-Landau notation.' Computer scientists adopted it to describe the upper-bound growth rate of algorithm running times.
visualDescription: A late 19th-century German mathematics textbook open to a page introducing the 'O' symbol in formal analysis, with modern algorithm complexity charts overlaid

quizQuestion: A binary heap (min-heap) always keeps the smallest element at its root. What is the time complexity of finding that minimum?
explanation: Finding the minimum in a min-heap (or maximum in a max-heap) is O(1) because it is always stored at the root. Inserting and deleting are O(log n) as elements may need to bubble up or down. Heaps power priority queues, Dijkstra's algorithm, and A* search.
visualDescription: A min-heap tree with the smallest value highlighted at the root, with O(1) labeled beside an arrow pointing directly to it
